chapterSummary.title: Fractions
chapterSummary.content: This chapter introduces students to the world of fractions. A fraction represents a part of a whole, written with a numerator (top number) and a denominator (bottom number). Students will learn what fractions mean, explore equivalent fractions that look different but show the same amount, and practise adding fractions with the same denominator. Through real-world examples like sharing pizza, measuring ingredients, and telling time, students will see that fractions are an important part of everyday life.
keyPoints: A fraction shows a part of a whole divided into equal parts
The numerator tells how many parts you have; the denominator tells total parts
Equivalent fractions look different but represent the same value
To find equivalent fractions, multiply or divide both numbers by the same amount
When adding fractions with the same denominator, add the numerators and keep the denominator
learningObjectives: Understand what fractions are and identify numerator and denominator
Represent fractions using pictures, shapes, and number lines
Simplify fractions to their lowest form
Add fractions that have the same denominator

curatedNotes.title: Draw It Out!
curatedNotes.content: The best way to understand fractions is to draw them. Take a circle or rectangle and divide it into equal parts. Shade in the parts your fraction talks about. For example, to show 3/4, draw a shape with 4 equal parts and colour 3 of them. Seeing fractions makes them much easier to understand!

curatedNotes.title: Common Mistake: Unequal Parts
curatedNotes.content: Remember, fractions only work with EQUAL parts! If you cut a pizza into 4 pieces but one piece is bigger than the others, you can't call each piece 1/4. Always check that all parts are the same size before writing a fraction.

curatedNotes.title: Kitchen Fractions Practice
curatedNotes.content: Next time you're in the kitchen, look at measuring cups and spoons. You'll find 1/2 cup, 1/4 cup, and 1/3 cup measures. Try helping with a recipe and practise reading fractions. Ask: which is bigger, 1/2 cup or 1/4 cup? Real-life practice makes fractions stick!
